title: Ankara Courts Cairo on Defense as Navy Returns to Latakia, Assassination Plot Revealed
subhead: Türkiye signed a defense industry letter of intent with Egypt and sent warships to Syria's Latakia for the first time since the civil war, while Israeli media reported an Iranian assassination plan targeting Trump during the Ankara summit. At home, a mass FETO sweep coincided with the July 15 anniversary and markets slid on renewed Hormuz fears.

sections.heading: Türkiye and Egypt Sign Defense Industry Letter of Intent
sections.body:
Türkiye and Egypt signed a letter of intent (LoI) on Monday to establish a framework for defense industry cooperation, Turkish Defense Industries Secretary Haluk Gorgun announced. Egyptian Defense and Military Production Minister Lt. Gen. Ashraf Salem Zaher visited Ankara and met with Gorgun at the Secretariat of Defense Industries, where the two sides discussed possible areas for joint capability development.

Gorgun said on X that the LoI 'will define the framework of our cooperation in the period ahead,' adding that closer defense ties between Ankara and Cairo 'will contribute to regional security and mutual capability development.' Earlier the same day, Turkish National Defense Minister Yasar Guler hosted Zaher with a military ceremony at the ministry, followed by closed-door talks. Al-Monitor reported the two countries also signed a separate LoI on joint military capability development, signaling a broadening of ties beyond the purely symbolic.

The agreement marks a tangible step in the ongoing rapprochement between Ankara and Cairo, which has progressed from restoring ambassadorial relations in 2023 to defense-level consultations. An Israeli National News analysis described the emerging alignment as a 'pragmatic, transactional alliance' between two regimes setting aside past grievances, though significant differences remain over Libya and the eastern Mediterranean.
sections.whyItMatters: The LoI moves Türkiye-Egypt normalization from diplomatic gestures into the defense-industrial domain — a qualitatively different stage. If joint capability development follows, it would reshape power dynamics across the eastern Mediterranean and North Africa.

sections.heading: Turkish Navy Returns to Latakia — First Visit Since Syrian Civil War
sections.body:
The Turkish Naval Forces sent ships to Syria's Latakia Port, marking the first such visit since the start of the Syrian civil war, Türkiye's Defense Ministry announced Monday. Naval Forces Commander Adm. Ercument Tatlioglu was aboard the frigate TCG Istanbul during the visit, according to the ministry's statement.

The port call is the most visible military-to-military signal Ankara has sent to the new Syrian administration of President Ahmad al-Sharaa since Assad's ouster in late 2024. It follows growing Turkish engagement with Damascus, including diplomatic contacts and security cooperation aimed at stabilizing the border region.

The symbolism is hard to miss: a Turkish warship docking at Latakia — long a stronghold of Russian naval influence and the Assad regime — signals both Moscow's diminished footprint and Ankara's willingness to project naval power into post-Assad Syria's littoral space.
sections.whyItMatters: A Turkish naval presence at Latakia rewrites the eastern Mediterranean's post-Assad security map. It signals to Moscow, Tehran, and Washington alike that Ankara considers itself a stakeholder in Syria's maritime future.

sections.heading: Israeli Media: Iran Plotted to Assassinate Trump During Ankara Summit
sections.body:
Israeli media reported Sunday that Iran allegedly planned to assassinate U.S. President Donald Trump on Turkish soil during last week's NATO summit in Ankara. According to Israeli broadcaster Channel 12, senior Iranian officials viewed Trump's Ankara visit as a 'one-time opportunity.' A Western intelligence agency — identified by Channel 13 as Israel's — detected the plan and warned U.S. authorities in advance, leading to a switch of presidential aircraft for the departure.

Channel 13 cited a senior Iranian official as saying: 'This is our opportunity, there will be no better opportunity than this,' in reference to Trump's planned visit. The New York Times had earlier reported that Trump departed Ankara aboard his older Air Force One rather than the newer Qatar-gifted plane, following a Secret Service security recommendation. U.S. Ambassador to Israel Mike Huckabee confirmed the reports on Fox News, stating that 'Israeli intelligence passed information to Trump and his administration about a very specific assassination attempt that the Iranians tried to carry out against the president.'

The allegations come amid an already volatile U.S.-Iran relationship following the spring conflict and subsequent ceasefire. Neither Iranian nor Turkish officials have publicly commented on the Israeli media reports.
sections.whyItMatters: If verified, the plot would represent one of the most serious Iranian state-sponsored assassination attempts against a sitting U.S. president — and it unfolded on Turkish soil during a summit Ankara hosted. The episode raises difficult questions about Türkiye's ability to secure major events and deepens the stakes of Tehran's shadow war with Washington.

sections.heading: Mass FETO Operation Targets 968 Suspects Across All 81 Provinces
sections.body:
Türkiye's Interior and Justice ministries launched a coordinated operation targeting 968 suspects linked to what Ankara calls the Fethullah Gulen Terrorist Organization (FETO) across all 81 provinces on Monday, coinciding with the 10th anniversary of the July 15, 2016 failed coup attempt.

Interior Minister Mustafa Ciftci and Justice Minister Akin Gurlek issued a joint statement describing the sweep as part of 'a major cleansing effort across all institutions' under President Erdogan's leadership. The highest number of suspects was concentrated in Ankara, Izmir, and Istanbul. The statement vowed to 'never allow any terrorist organization or center of tutelage to threaten the will of our esteemed nation.' Separately, Turkish intelligence agency MIT captured and repatriated Daesh suspect Talip Guler from a conflict zone, identifying him as the brother of a former Daesh 'Türkiye Province' chief who had been captured in a 2021 operation. Guler operated under the code name 'Abdusselam Turki' within the group's so-called Faruk Office network.
sections.whyItMatters: The scale of the FETO operation — nearly 1,000 targets in a single day — underscores that the July 15 anniversary remains a central organizing principle for the Turkish security apparatus a decade on. The simultaneous Daesh capture signals Ankara's dual-track approach to domestic counterterrorism.

sections.heading: Markets Slide on Hormuz Fears as Current Account Deficit Narrows
sections.body:
Türkiye's BIST 100 index fell over 2% at its intraday low on Monday, its steepest decline since late May, after Tehran announced it had again closed the Strait of Hormuz and Brent crude briefly surged past $80 before settling around $78.2 a barrel. Financial stocks dropped 2.5% and the banking index lost 2.3%, with leasing and factoring companies plunging 9.5%. A total of 22 of 31 sector indexes traded lower.

The selloff came despite relatively positive macro data. The current account deficit narrowed to $1.5 billion in May from $5.7 billion in April, though it came in above the $1.3 billion market forecast. The improvement was largely calendar-driven — national holidays and Eid al-Adha compressed activity during the month. The 12-month rolling deficit stood at $37.3 billion.

JPMorgan assessed that the tourism season would keep pressure off the lira through Q4, with Q1 tourism revenue up 4.2% year-on-year to $9.9 billion. However, the bank projected the annual current account deficit would widen to $48 billion (2.8% of GDP) by year-end, with inflation ending 2026 at 29% and the dollar/lira at 51.4. CBRT gross reserves stood at $159.7 billion as of July 3, down $50.6 billion from pre-war levels.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz shock hit Turkish markets at a moment when macro data was actually improving. JPMorgan's $48 billion deficit forecast underscores that the external balance is structurally strained regardless of tourism inflows — and renewed energy disruption would widen the gap further.

sections.heading: EU Appoints Cyprus Special Envoy; Ankara Calls It EU's Internal Matter
sections.body:
European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen announced that the EU will designate Vice President Raffaele Fitto as its special representative for Cyprus, citing 'renewed momentum' in the process following a phone call with U.N. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres. Von der Leyen said the opportunity should be seized and that the Commission stands ready to support efforts with 'all necessary assistance and tools.'

Türkiye's Foreign Ministry spokesman Oncu Keceli described the appointment as 'an internal matter of the European Union,' while reiterating that the bloc 'lost its neutrality on the Cyprus issue in 2004' by admitting the Greek Cypriot administration despite its rejection of the U.N.'s Annan Plan. The TRNC Foreign Ministry called the appointment 'provocative' and 'illegal,' saying it was made without the consent of the Turkish Cypriot side and that EU pressure efforts were 'doomed to fail.'

Ankara said it expects the new envoy to recognize that a settlement can only be reached through negotiations between 'two sovereign and equal states' — language that signals no shift from Türkiye's long-standing position.
sections.whyItMatters: The EU is inserting itself more directly into the Cyprus file at a moment of renewed diplomatic activity, but both Ankara and the TRNC immediately dismissed the move as biased. The gap between Brussels' framing and Ankara's 'two-state' reality remains as wide as ever.

sections.heading: Erdogan Approval at 44.5% as CHP Grapples With Leadership Crisis
sections.body:
President Erdogan's job approval stood at 44.5% with 51% disapproval, according to a Metropoll Research survey conducted June 15–25. Approval among AK Party voters was 81.1%, while MHP voters backed him at 60%. Among CHP voters, disapproval reached 83.4%.

The poll comes amid an ongoing leadership crisis in the main opposition CHP, triggered by an Ankara court's annulment of the party's 2023 congress over alleged procedural irregularities — a ruling that reinstated former leader Kemal Kilicdaroglu as interim head. The CHP has denied misconduct allegations. The same Metropoll data showed CHP support slipping after the court ruling, narrowing the gap between the ruling coalition and the opposition.

The 44.5% figure is marginally below the level Erdogan would likely need for a first-round victory in any future presidential contest, but it reflects a modest post-summit bump from the diplomatic visibility the NATO hosting provided.
sections.whyItMatters: The CHP's internal crisis has given Erdogan breathing room even as inflation and living costs remain the dominant voter concern. But 44.5% leaves no margin for error — and the opposition's disarray could shift quickly if the court ruling is overturned on appeal.

bottomLine: July 14 saw Ankara extend its regional footprint in two directions at once — signing a defense industry LoI with Egypt and sending a warship to Latakia for the first time since the Syrian war began. Both moves signal that Türkiye is consolidating post-Assad, post-rift relationships from the eastern Mediterranean to the Levant. Domestically, the July 15 anniversary drove a near-1,000-person FETO sweep and the MIT capture of a Daesh figure, while markets absorbed the latest Hormuz disruption and the current account showed modest improvement. The Iranian assassination plot allegation against Trump, if confirmed, will complicate Türkiye's positioning between Washington and Tehran for months to come.
sources: Türkiye Today, Al-Monitor, Israel National News, Forbes, France24, JPMorgan Research, Metropoll Research, To Vima, Channel 12 (Israel), Channel 13 (Israel), New York Times, Fox News, Middle East Eye, Anadolu Agency
